cases.passage: The Indian National Congress was a dominant force in Indian politics after independence. However, its leadership faced internal conflicts and challenges from various regional parties. The split in the Indian National Congress in 1969 led to the formation of two factions - Congress (R) and Congress (O). This marked the beginning of the end of the Congress system. The Congress (R) faction, led by Indira Gandhi, adopted a more authoritarian approach, while the Congress (O) faction, led by Morarji Desai, emphasized the need for a more decentralized and democratic approach. The divide within the Congress weakened its ability to govern effectively, leading to the collapse of the Congress system.

questions.q: What led to the formation of two factions of the Indian National Congress?
opts: (B) External pressure from other political parties
questions.explanation: Internal conflicts within the party led to the formation of two factions of the Indian National Congress.

questions.q: Which faction of the Indian National Congress emphasized the need for a more decentralized and democratic approach?
questions.explanation: The Congress (O) faction emphasized the need for a more decentralized and democratic approach.

questions.q: What was the outcome of the divide within the Congress?
opts: (D) Rise of a new political party
questions.explanation: The divide within the Congress weakened its ability to govern effectively, leading to the collapse of the Congress system.
Indira Gandhi led the Congress (R) faction.

questions.q: In which year did the Indian National Congress split?
questions.explanation: The Indian National Congress split in 1969.

cases.passage: The Congress system, which was the dominant political system in India after independence, faced several challenges. One of the major challenges was the rise of regional parties, which contested elections and won seats in the Lok Sabha. The Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam (DMK) in Tamil Nadu and the Telugu Desam Party (TDP) in Andhra Pradesh were two such regional parties. These parties challenged the Congress system by offering alternative governance and policies to the people. The Congress system was also challenged by the emergence of the Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP), which was a right-wing party that opposed the Congress's socialist policies.

questions.q: Which regional parties challenged the Congress system in Tamil Nadu and Andhra Pradesh?
questions.explanation: The DMK in Tamil Nadu and the TDP in Andhra Pradesh challenged the Congress system.

questions.q: What was the main policy difference between the Congress and the BJP?
questions.explanation: The main policy difference between the Congress and the BJP was social policies, with the BJP opposing the Congress's socialist policies.

questions.q: What was the impact of the emergence of regional parties on the Congress system?
opts: (C) No impact on the Congress system
(D) Rise of a new national party
questions.explanation: The emergence of regional parties weakened the Congress system by offering alternative governance and policies to the people.

questions.q: What was the main characteristic of the Congress system in India after independence?
questions.explanation: The Congress system was the dominant political system in India after independence.

questions.q: Which party emerged as a major challenger to the Congress system?
questions.explanation: The Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP) emerged as a major challenger to the Congress system.
